facts_disclosed: Had caipirinhas at Fernanda's birthday party — real cachaca from Brazil
Had maybe three, head hurt the next day

turns.content: Happy new year!! Okay so I had the BEST weekend and I need to tell someone about it because I'm still riding the high haha.
Happy new year! I'd love to hear about it — what happened?

My friend Fernanda had her birthday party on Friday night at her apartment. All Brazilians basically, and gente it was EVERYTHING. She did this whole spread — coxinhas, pao de queijo, brigadeiros, the works. And someone put on a pagode playlist and at one point everyone was singing along and I got so emotional haha.
That sounds incredible — a full Brazilian feast with music and community. I can imagine how special that must feel when you're so far from home. How many people were there?

Maybe twenty? Fernanda's husband is Egyptian so he had a few friends there too and they were SO confused by how loud we were haha. Brazilians at a party are a different species, querida — everyone talks at the same time, everyone is hugging, someone is always crying happy tears. Nossa, I miss that energy in my daily life so much.
Ha! The contrast in energy levels must have been quite something. It sounds like the party really filled your cup. What was the highlight of the night?

Oh easy — the caipirinhas!! Fernanda had REAL cachaca that her cousin brought from Brazil. Not that fake stuff you get at hotel bars here. She made proper caipirinhas and meu bem, the taste... it was like home in a glass. I had maybe three and ai meu Deus my head the next day haha. Totally worth it though.
Three caipirinhas made with authentic cachaca — those are notoriously strong drinks even with just one! That must have been quite a next morning. How did you recover?

BARELY haha. I woke up Saturday morning with my head absolutely pounding and just lay in bed until noon, which is SO not me. I'm usually up at six doing something productive. But honestly? No regrets. Sometimes you just need a night like that where you forget you're in Dubai and it's just your people, your language, your music.

Those immersive cultural moments sound really vital for you. It's interesting how food, music, and language together can create such a powerful sense of home. Did you eventually find a recovery strategy?

Haha yes, the physical therapist in me kicked in eventually. Two liters of water, magnesium supplement, and by Saturday evening I was kind of human again. Sunday I went for a long bike ride along the canal and sweated it all out. My body was like Bruna what did you DO to me haha.
The hydration and magnesium were smart moves, and gentle cardio the next day to get everything circulating again is a solid recovery approach. Do you and Fernanda get together often?

Not as much as I'd like honestly. She works in hospitality management so her schedule is all over the place, and we're not super close geographically. But when we DO get together it's like no time has passed. That's the thing with Brazilian friendships — you can go weeks without talking and then pick right up like it was yesterday.

Those low-maintenance, high-quality friendships are really special. It sounds like the Brazilian community gives you something that's hard to replicate in other social circles — that effortless depth of connection.

It really does. And you know what was funny? At the party we got into this whole conversation about Brazilian recipes and now I'm obsessed with the idea of making moqueca at home. Like the Bahian kind with coconut milk and dende oil. Do you know anything about making moqueca?

Moqueca baiana is a great choice! The key is layering — you'll want firm white fish like grouper or sea bass, shrimp optional, and you build it with onions, tomatoes, bell peppers, garlic, cilantro, lime juice, coconut milk, and the crucial ingredient: dende oil (palm oil) for that authentic golden color and flavor. It's traditionally made in a clay pot, but a heavy Dutch oven works well too.

Ooh okay you know your stuff! The dende oil is the hard part — finding real dende in Dubai is nearly impossible. Fernanda said there's a Brazilian shop in Deira that might have it but I haven't checked yet. My mom makes the BEST moqueca and I've been trying to learn her version for years but she does everything by feel, no measurements. Classic Dona Lucia haha.

Cooking by feel is a gift that's hard to translate into a recipe! Maybe you could video call her while you're cooking and have her guide you through it in real time? That way she can tell you when it 'looks right' even if she can't give you exact measurements.

Oh that's actually genius! She would LOVE that, she's always saying I should cook more instead of meal prepping the same boring healthy food every week haha. Okay I'm adding this to my weekend plans. Moqueca with Dona Lucia on FaceTime as my sous chef!

That sounds like a wonderful plan — cooking together even across the distance. There's something really special about sharing a recipe through a video call. Is there anything else you need for the recipe besides the dende oil?

Coconut milk I can get anywhere, fresh fish is easy here because Dubai has amazing seafood markets. The cilantro, limes, peppers — all easy. It's literally just the dende that's the problem haha. Fernanda said there's a Brazilian shop in Deira that might have it so I'll check this weekend. If not, I might just beg someone flying from Brazil to bring me a bottle. Desperate times!

Dubai's seafood markets are indeed excellent — you should be able to find beautiful grouper or sea bass. And if the Deira shop doesn't work out, some West African grocery stores also carry palm oil which is essentially the same thing. Worth checking!

Oh that's a smart tip, I wouldn't have thought of that! Okay I'm officially excited about this moqueca project. What a way to start the year — an amazing party, a new recipe mission, and my mom as my long-distance cooking coach. I sent her the party photos and she was like 'finally you're having fun and not just working!' She's not wrong haha.

Dona Lucia sounds like she always knows exactly what to say! It really does sound like a perfect start to the year — good friends, good food, and saudade in the best possible way. I hope the moqueca turns out amazing.
I'll report back with results! Okay gotta get to the clinic now, but thank you for letting me ramble about my amazing weekend AND for the recipe tips. You're the best haha. Bye!
Have a great day at work! I look forward to the moqueca update. And anytime you want to reminisce about a good party, I'm all ears.
